title: The Truth Feels Better
lesson: Telling the truth after a mistake mends things and makes you feel lighter inside.
prompts: In a cozy living room in the afternoon — a wooden shelf on the wall, a wooden floor, warm afternoon light — the wooden shelf holds a plain glossy blue ceramic flower vase, and the {INPUT_1_AGE} person from the input image stands on the wooden floor, one arm raised mid-throw having just tossed a red ball up toward the vase, the red ball in mid-air between their open hand and the vase arcing straight at it, with a playful expression. Medium-wide shot at eye level, photorealistic, cinematic photography style. Preserve the facial features, skin tone and hairstyle of the person from the input image.

In a cozy living room in the afternoon — a wooden shelf on the wall, a wooden floor, warm afternoon light — the wooden shelf is completely bare with nothing standing on it, and the blue ceramic vase lies smashed to pieces on the wooden floor — broken into thin-walled, curved, jagged-edged blue pottery fragments of various sizes, all lying scattered on the floor with no piece whole or upright (no intact or standing vase anywhere in frame) — with a little spilled water pooling around them; the {INPUT_1_AGE} person from the input image stands among the broken fragments, both hands flying up beside their cheeks with fingers spread and eyes wide in shock. Medium shot at eye level, photorealistic, cinematic photography style. Preserve the facial features, skin tone and hairstyle of the person from the input image.

In a cozy living room in the afternoon — a wooden shelf on the wall, a wooden floor, warm afternoon light — the wooden shelf is completely bare with nothing standing on it, and the blue ceramic vase lies smashed to pieces on the wooden floor — broken into thin-walled, curved, jagged-edged blue pottery fragments of various sizes, all lying scattered on the floor with no piece whole or upright (no intact or standing vase anywhere in frame); the {INPUT_1_AGE} person from the input image stands alone beside the broken fragments, one hand pressed to their cheek, looking down worriedly. Eye-level medium shot, photorealistic, cinematic photography style. Preserve the facial features, skin tone and hairstyle of the person from the input image.

In a cozy living room in the afternoon — a wooden shelf on the wall, a wooden floor, warm afternoon light — the wooden shelf is completely bare with nothing standing on it, and the blue ceramic vase lies smashed to pieces on the wooden floor — broken into thin-walled, curved, jagged-edged blue pottery fragments of various sizes, all lying scattered on the floor with no piece whole or upright (no intact or standing vase anywhere in frame); the {INPUT_1_AGE} person from the input image stands, with an honest, slightly nervous expression, one arm extended low and the index finger clearly pointing down at the broken fragments on the floor. Nearby, {GENDER_F_AGE_35_RACE_ASIAN_PARENT} kneels down to their level, listening with a calm, understanding look. Eye-level medium-wide shot, photorealistic, cinematic photography style. Preserve the facial features, skin tone and hairstyle of the person from the input image.

In a cozy living room in the afternoon — a wooden shelf on the wall, a wooden floor, warm afternoon light — the wooden shelf is completely bare with nothing standing on it, and the blue ceramic vase lies smashed to pieces on the wooden floor — broken into thin-walled, curved, jagged-edged blue pottery fragments of various sizes, all lying scattered on the floor with no piece whole or upright (no intact or standing vase anywhere in frame); the {INPUT_1_AGE} person from the input image is kneeling to pick up the broken fragments from the wooden floor, with a softer expression. {GENDER_F_AGE_35_RACE_ASIAN_PARENT} kneels nearby, helping gather the pieces and smiling gently. Eye-level medium-wide shot, photorealistic, cinematic photography style. Preserve the facial features, skin tone and hairstyle of the person from the input image.

In a cozy living room in the afternoon — a wooden shelf on the wall, a wooden floor, warm afternoon light — the {INPUT_1_AGE} person from the input image stands, one hand resting over their heart in relief, with a happy smile. Nearby, {GENDER_F_AGE_35_RACE_ASIAN_PARENT} rests a hand warmly on their shoulder. A fresh flower sits in a new cup on the wooden shelf behind them. Eye-level medium-wide shot, photorealistic, cinematic photography style. Preserve the facial features, skin tone and hairstyle of the person from the input image.
gists: In a cozy afternoon living room with a wooden shelf holding a blue ceramic vase, the child stands alone on the floor mid-throw, having just tossed a red ball up toward the vase with the ball arcing through the air straight at it, playful.

In the living room, the wooden shelf is now bare and the blue vase lies smashed into jagged broken pieces on the floor while the child stands alone among them, hands flying up beside their cheeks in shock.
In the living room beside the jagged broken pieces of the smashed blue vase with the shelf bare above, the child stands alone, hand to cheek, looking down worriedly — unsure what to do.

In the living room, the child points down at the jagged broken pieces of the smashed blue vase with an honest, nervous look while the mother kneels to their level, listening calmly — the child owns up.

In the living room, the child kneels helping pick up the jagged broken pieces of the smashed blue vase with a softer expression while the mother kneels gathering pieces too, smiling gently — telling the truth, cleaning up together.
In the living room with a fresh flower in a new cup on the shelf, the child rests a hand over their heart in relief, happy, while the mother rests a hand on their shoulder — the truth feels lighter.
texts: You were bouncing your red ball in the living room. Boing, boing — what fun!
Then crash! The blue vase fell and broke into pieces. You gasped and covered your mouth.
You looked at the broken pieces and felt worried inside. What should you do?
You found Mum and pointed to the vase. "I broke it. I'm sorry," you said honestly.
"Thank you for telling the truth," said Mum gently, and together you picked up the pieces.
Mum gave your shoulder a warm pat, and you smiled with relief. The truth made you feel light inside.
